button.description: Triggers actions or events when clicked, supporting multiple visual styles and semantic colors.
button.overview: Button provides interactive elements for triggering actions in forms, dialogs, navigation, and other contexts. It supports three visual variants: default with solid background, outline with bordered style, and ghost with transparent background. Nine semantic colors are available including main, accent, success, error, warning, info, dark, light, and inherit. Four size options (default, sm, lg, icon) accommodate different layout needs. The asChild prop enables polymorphic rendering to wrap links or other elements while maintaining button styling.
button.usage: Use Button for primary actions, form submissions, and navigation triggers. For basic usage, render with children text and optional variant or color props. Apply outline variant for secondary actions and ghost variant for tertiary or subtle interactions. Use semantic colors to convey meaning: success for confirmations, error for destructive actions, warning for cautionary operations. The icon size creates circular buttons suitable for icon-only content. Combine with asChild prop to render as anchor tags for navigation while preserving button appearance.
button.accessibility: This component meets the following accessibility requirements. For keyboard support, buttons are focusable via Tab and activated with Enter or Space keys. Regarding semantic HTML, the component renders as a native button element by default with proper type attributes. For focus indicators, visible focus outlines appear on keyboard navigation. Disabled state uses pointer-events-none and reduced opacity to indicate non-interactive status. When using asChild with links, ensure proper aria-labels describe the action clearly.
doList: Use descriptive text that explains the action
Apply semantic colors to convey button purpose
Maintain consistent sizing within related button groups
Include visible focus states for keyboard users
Use asChild with proper aria attributes for link buttons
dontList: Use vague labels like 'Click here' without context
Mix multiple variant styles in the same button group
Apply icon size without providing accessible labels
Disable buttons without explaining why to users
Use color alone to convey meaning without text

relatedComponents.name: Icon
relatedComponents.description: Combine with Button for icon-enhanced actions

relatedComponents.name: Dialog
relatedComponents.description: Use Button to trigger and control dialog actions

relatedComponents.description: Use Button as dropdown menu triggers

avatar.description: Displays a user or entity image with fallback support for missing or loading states.
avatar.overview: Avatar represents users, organizations, or entities in a compact visual format. It consists of three parts: Root container for layout, Image for displaying the profile picture, and Fallback for showing initials or placeholder content when the image is unavailable. The component supports responsive sizing through className props and maintains a circular shape by default.
avatar.usage: Use Avatar to represent users in navigation headers, comments, user lists, or profile sections. For basic usage, wrap Avatar.Image with the Avatar root component and provide src and alt props. When images may fail to load, include Avatar.Fallback as a sibling to display initials or an icon. Control size by passing Tailwind width and height classes to the className prop. Combine multiple Avatars in a group to show team members or collaborators.
avatar.accessibility: This component meets the following accessibility requirements. For screen reader support, Avatar.Image accepts an alt prop that describes the user or entity. Regarding semantic structure, the component uses span elements appropriately and maintains proper DOM hierarchy. For image states, the fallback mechanism ensures meaningful content is always displayed. Decorative avatars should use an empty alt attribute while informative avatars require descriptive alt text.
doList: Provide descriptive alt text for user identification
Use Avatar.Fallback as a sibling with Avatar.Image
Apply consistent sizing across similar contexts
Use initials or icons as meaningful fallback content
Ensure fallback text has sufficient contrast
dontList: Omit alt text for informative avatars
Use decorative images without empty alt attributes
Apply sizes that make content illegible
Use low-contrast colors for fallback backgrounds
Nest Avatar components within each other

relatedComponents.name: Button
relatedComponents.description: Use with Avatar for user action buttons

relatedComponents.name: DropdownMenu
relatedComponents.description: Combine Avatar with dropdown for user menus

dialog.description: Displays modal content that overlays the page, requiring user interaction before returning to the main content.
dialog.overview: Dialog presents focused content in an overlay that requires user attention. It consists of a trigger element that opens the dialog, an overlay that dims the background, and a content area with optional title and description. The component supports controlled and uncontrolled states through the open prop. A close button is automatically included with proper accessibility labeling. The dialog centers itself on screen and prevents interaction with background content until dismissed.
dialog.usage: Use Dialog for confirmations, forms, alerts, or any content requiring focused user attention. For basic usage, provide a trigger element and content prop. Add title and description props for better context and accessibility. Use controlled mode with the open prop for programmatic control over dialog state. Combine with Button component for consistent trigger styling. Include action buttons within content for user responses like confirm or cancel. For form dialogs, place the form element inside the content prop with appropriate submit handling.
dialog.accessibility: This component meets the following accessibility requirements. For focus management, focus moves to the dialog content when opened and returns to the trigger element when closed. Regarding keyboard navigation, pressing Escape closes the dialog and Tab cycles through focusable elements within the dialog. The component uses semantic HTML with Dialog.Title and Dialog.Description for screen reader announcements. An aria-label on the close button clearly indicates its purpose. The overlay prevents interaction with background content ensuring modal behavior. Screen readers announce the dialog title and description when opened.
doList: Provide a descriptive title for context
Include clear action buttons for user responses
Use description prop to explain the dialog purpose
Ensure trigger element has descriptive text
Keep dialog content focused and concise
dontList: Omit close functionality from the dialog

relatedComponents.name: Button
relatedComponents.description: Use as trigger elements and action buttons within dialogs

relatedComponents.name: Icon
relatedComponents.description: Provides the close button icon within the dialog

Official documentation for the underlying dialog primitive component

concept_description: B.G. Neo Grid Design System is a design foundation that steadily advances toward the future while retaining the warmth of a sunlit, tranquil room. Within a comfortable retro environment, it establishes a platform that connects the present with what comes next by combining functionality and scalability designed for the coming era. It is a new design framework that blends the refined structure of a near-future metropolis with the lived-in retro vintage charm of the 80s and 90s. Reminiscent of a modest retro apartment room adorned with well-worn tin decor and indoor plant greenery, it conveys a modern outline that looks confidently toward the future.

introduction.title: B.G. Neo Grid Design System

items.heading: The Warmth of a Futuristic Metropolis
items.text: Combines the sleek feeling of a futuristic urban environment with the cozy warmth of materials such as wood and tin. It incorporates the familiar atmosphere of everyday furniture and interiors into a grid layout, creating a gentle and welcoming ambiance that isn't too sterile.

items.heading: A Retro Space Illuminated by Warm Light
items.text: Utilizes retro furniture and decor with a tone and manner centered on bright light. Indoor plants, soft wood hues, and subtle metal accents balance retro charm and sophistication.

items.heading: A Simple Grid Structure Resembling Pixel Art
items.text: Designed such that small components gather to form a grid that drives overall growth, akin to pixels forming pixel art; the combination of discrete parts creates meaning and value.

items.heading: Harmony Between Nature and Industrial
items.text: Integrates industrial elements like tin storage and steel shelving with natural materials and greenery to achieve a perfect balance of softness. The combination of urban, hard elements with a soothing, home-like atmosphere creates a unique interior aesthetic.

introduction.itemAlt: An abstract image representing the composition of component data.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: B.G. Neo Grid Design System strives for comfort and functionality. We focus on the following points to deliver a unique world that fuses futuristic flair with a retro material aesthetic to all users.
accessibility.imageAlt: An illustration representing the composition of component data.

items.title: Web Accessibility and WCAG Compliance
paragraphs: Aim to achieve WCAG 2.1 AA or higher while incorporating retro design, ensuring proper contrast, structure, and ease of navigation.
Implement machine-readable code, appropriate semantics, and focus management as the baseline, integrating accessibility requirements into UI component design.

items.title: Color and Contrast
paragraphs: Adopt an approach where small elements combine to form the whole, assessing and improving accessibility on a component-by-component basis.
Continuously update to meet accessibility standards even as new features and design elements are introduced.

items.title: Text and Typography
paragraphs: While using near-futuristic and industrial elements, incorporate natural touches like wood textures or greenery to evoke warmth.
Ensure that warm color palettes and textures do not compromise legibility by strictly maintaining appropriate contrast ratios and color accessibility.

items.title: Interaction and Navigation
paragraphs: Incorporate design nuances reminiscent of retro decor and vintage furniture, complemented by brightness and soft saturation reminiscent of daylight.
Even with soft hues, ensure that text and icons remain legible through optimal contrast and color schemes.

items.heading: Basic Requirements
items.paragraph: Meet essential standards such as text/background contrast, font sizes, line spacing, etc. Ensure interactive elements are clearly visible with a minimum contrast ratio of 4.5:1.

items.heading: Screen Reader Support
items.paragraph: Structure HTML appropriately so that assistive technologies can accurately read content. Decorative images should have empty alt attributes, while informative images must have appropriate alt text.

items.heading: Keyboard Navigation
items.paragraph: Ensure all interactive elements (forms, buttons, links, modals, etc.) can be operated via keyboard. Set logical tab order and provide skip links to main content.

items.heading: Alternative Text for Images
items.paragraph: Exclude decorative images from screen reader narration and assign appropriate alt text to UI elements with functional images.

items.heading: Video and Audio
items.paragraph: Provide captions for videos and textual descriptions. Avoid autoplay for audio, and offer easy controls for volume adjustment and pausing.

items.heading: Forms
items.paragraph: Display clear labels for input fields, and communicate required fields and errors effectively. Implementations should accurately inform screen readers of any error messages.

items.heading: Layout Structure
items.paragraph: Organize information in a clear and logical hierarchy and ensure that the layout is user-friendly on mobile devices.

items.heading: Color Usage
items.paragraph: Set appropriate contrast between text and background, and consider diverse color perceptions for effective communication.

items.heading: Clear Writing
items.paragraph: Use simple language and clearly describe interactive processes. Maintain proper heading hierarchy and organize content effectively.

items.heading: Animation
items.paragraph: Use animations sparingly and provide options to pause when necessary. Avoid overly aggressive or flashing animations.

items.heading: Layout
items.paragraph: Structure header, main content, and footer appropriately to ensure mobile responsiveness and usability.

items.heading: Modal Dialogs
items.paragraph: When displaying modals, dim the background and manage focus appropriately with consideration for screen reader use.

items.heading: Notifications
items.paragraph: Ensure notifications are clearly visible and accurately communicated to screen readers.

items.heading: Slideshows
items.paragraph: Provide options to pause autoplay and allow manual control, ensuring that the current slide content is effectively conveyed to screen readers.

items.heading: Tooltips
items.paragraph: Clearly control the display of additional information to ensure that its visibility and hiding are well-managed for screen readers.

items.heading: Sorting Functionality
items.paragraph: Allow sorting to be operated via both mouse and keyboard, with clear indication of actionable areas and outcomes.

items.heading: Menus
items.paragraph: For mobile menus, provide appropriate labels and clearly indicate open or closed states.

items.heading: Page Transitions
items.paragraph: Always display the current location and clearly indicate the purpose of links and buttons. Notify users appropriately when the page content is updated.

responsive.title: Responsive

description.paragraph1: We aim to provide a UI that is comfortable across various screen sizes, offering a modern yet approachable design. The goal is to deliver a user-friendly and warm UI experience even on the latest devices, while always keeping accessibility (WCAG 2.1 AA and above) in mind as layouts adapt fluidly based on screen size.
description.paragraph2: Each component is designed to flexibly expand or contract according to screen size, maintaining a basic layout that facilitates easy element arrangement. Even on small screens, careful color usage and appropriate spacing ensure that the main content remains clear and readable.

responsive.heroImageAlt: An abstract image representing the data structure of a component

breakpoints.heading: Breakpoints
breakpoints.description: This design system sets key breakpoints based on screen size. On smaller screens, fewer elements are displayed per row with a vertical layout; on larger screens, multi-column layouts are utilized for a cohesive design.

row2.usage: Optimized for small tablets or large smartphones

row3.usage: For landscape tablets or small PCs

row4.usage: Suitable for laptops and typical desktops

row5.usage: For large desktops or TV displays

item1.heading: Design Consistency and Accessibility
paragraphs: On small screens, tactile colors and icons evoke a warm design; elements are appropriately omitted or rearranged to maintain overall consistency.

When view switching or wrapping occurs, careful considerations ensure that text size and tap targets do not become excessively small, and that focus movement or reading order remains intact during media query-triggered layout changes.

item2.heading: Grid System Design
paragraphs: For Small screens, a 4-column grid is used for simplicity; for Medium screens, a 6-column layout is employed; and for Large screens and beyond, a 12-column grid increases layout flexibility.

On smaller screens, sufficient spacing is maintained to prevent clutter while ensuring a minimum tap target size (approximately 44px square), and on larger screens, generous margins enhance visual clarity.
The layout is organized in a left-to-right reading order, with major elements such as hero images or cards spanning columns for impactful display.

item3.heading: Navigation Implementation
paragraphs: On Small screens, the global navigation is contained within a hamburger menu, with appropriate aria-labels and aria-expanded attributes ensuring accessibility.
On Medium screens and above, navigation items are displayed in a header or fixed sidebar, combining icons with text to ensure clear visibility.

item4.heading: Images and Decorative Elements
paragraphs: On small screens, decorative background images are managed so as not to obscure text, and decorative illustrations are provided with empty alt attributes.
Appropriate images are provided based on screen size, and decorative elements are carefully arranged to avoid interfering with UI components.
An overlay is used behind images to maintain proper contrast and accessibility.

item5.heading: Typography and Form Elements
paragraphs: Text size adjusts responsively, with automatic modifications to line spacing and letter spacing.
When using background colors, a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1 is maintained, with careful consideration for text wrapping on small screens.
Forms are designed to adapt their layout based on screen size, with clear and accessible error messages.

item6.heading: Component Implementation Points
paragraphs: Components such as carousels, modals, and notifications are designed with responsive display in mind, ensuring accessibility at all times.
Optimized media queries, refined animations, considerations for both touch and mouse interactions, and proper management of aria attributes all contribute to a UI catered to every user.
